Actually most do and I try to do the opposite. you look at like magic of thinking big and psycho cybernetics Those guys most of their stories were unattributed I don't think you want to do a lot of it But I think if you mix it in with fully identified stories you can get away with one or two without having a believability problem um Page 290 is an example of telling a story about somebody else instead of yourself and making that work 292 is a reference to a And I'm going to give you the first person version of it This is a story probably half of the speakers in America have attributed and told first person as their own This is the I'm the one in charge of the butter story is is the punchline to this story And I have no idea if it's ever been legitimate or who actually Told it first and who stole it I have the first person version of it for you from Glenn Turner, which I'll get copied and give to you But this is so when I want to use one like that that I know has been abused I Won't risk telling it first person Because it's been hoared around too much But it still is such a good story I'll disclose that I'm telling an old story that a lot of people have used and I'll tell it that way I think by the way I See people a lot Using stuff that really does like has been identified to somebody Or has been hoared around a lot one way or the other and not giving credit and or risking appropriating it And I think that's dumb you know because Sure, most people aren't going to catch it, but the one who does catch it Really immediately thinks a lot less of you and it kind of undermines everything else that you do And I don't think you gain much from it I think it's just it serves the same purpose to say this came from Jim Marone as it does to make believe it came from you 293 is the
